_meta.fetchedBy: Claude — direct fetch of NREL ATB and EIA Construction Cost pages, plus WebSearch

primarySources.type: EIA Construction Cost Data — Generators Installed in 2023
primarySources.criticalFinding: Fuel cells are NOT listed among 2023 generator-cost technologies. Confirmed by direct fetch. EIA's notes explicitly state: 'Some generation technologies were excluded from this analysis to avoid disclosure of individual company data.' Because Bloom Energy dominates US SOFC deployment, aggregate fuel-cell capex would reveal Bloom's data — so EIA suppresses it. This is a structural ceiling on public capex transparency, not a fetch limitation.

primarySources.applicability_note: Confirmed by direct fetch — NREL ATB 2024 Electricity tracks: Coal, Natural Gas, Nuclear, Biopower, Geothermal, Hydropower, Pumped Storage, Solar PV (utility/commercial/residential), CSP, Wind (Onshore/Offshore), Battery Storage. Stationary fuel cells are NOT in this list. Transportation ATB has fuel cells but for vehicles, not stationary power. → ATB cannot supply our SOFC capex baseline.

primarySources.type: DOE Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies Office — published cost targets
primarySources.applicability_note: DOE publishes target/projection cost numbers ($/kW), not realized capex. Useful as forward-looking anchor.
FY2024 product revenue $1.085B; if MW shipped is disclosed in MD&A, implied price per kW can be derived. Direct 10-K text extraction is the next step.

_meta.verificationNote: Confirmed that the two most obvious public sources (NREL ATB electricity, EIA Construction Cost Data) do NOT publish realized US fuel-cell capex. Range below is synthesized from industry reporting and vendor disclosures; treated as a planning estimate, NOT an authoritative point estimate. Bloom 10-K MD&A is the remaining unexplored path to derive an implied price per kW from FY2024 product revenue / MW shipped.
_meta.nextRefresh: 2026-12 (EIA 2024 release in Sep 2026; Bloom FY2025 10-K analysis sooner)

structural_finding_2026_05_28.headline: Public US fuel-cell capex data is not transparently published in standard EIA/NREL sources because of vendor concentration (mostly Bloom Energy).
structural_finding_2026_05_28.implication_for_tool: Our fuel-cell capex assumption is necessarily a synthesized range, NOT a single authoritative figure pulled from a government table. We label it as such, make it editable, and note the underlying limitation in the tool's methodology.
what_we_can_do: Use Bloom Energy 10-K disclosed product revenue and (when available) MW shipped to derive an implied $/kW
Cite DOE target/projection numbers as forward-looking anchors
Cite EIA's expected-but-suppressed status to explain why no clean public number exists
When customers have site-specific quotes, use those as overrides — that becomes a moat as we accumulate quotes over time (clean-room aggregation; not vendor-confidential disclosure)
what_we_cannot_do: Pull a single authoritative US fuel-cell capex from a clean public table — it does not exist
Trust vendor brochure numbers as actual delivered cost (typically excludes site work, interconnection, soft costs)

current_tool_default._status: Planning estimate. To be cross-checked against Bloom 10-K implied $/kW once MW shipped is extracted. User-editable in the Assumptions panel.
current_tool_default._basisDocumented: Mid-range of $3,500-5,000/kW window synthesized from industry reporting and case studies.

typical_ranges_2025_2026._source: Synthesized from public reporting and Bloom Energy 10-K financial structure (gross margin trend); not a single authoritative source

fuelcell_SOFC_USD_per_kW.notes: Wide range reflects (a) order size — larger orders get discounted (b) contract structure — outright purchase vs Bloom EaaS PPA vs hybrid (c) site complexity — interconnection, soft costs vary (d) the $5,000+ upper end includes turnkey installations with extensive site work.

typical_ranges_2025_2026._caveat: Real vendor pricing varies substantially. For any specific opportunity, the only reliable figure is a vendor quote. This range is for screening, not procurement.

bloom_FY2024_implied_economics._status: Computed indirectly from press release / 10-K disclosed figures — not a primary published number
bloom_FY2024_implied_economics.FY2024_product_gross_margin: Approx 36-37% (derived from total cost of revenue product line: $685.8M cost vs $1.085B revenue = ~36.8% gross margin on product)
bloom_FY2024_implied_economics._interpretation: If Bloom maintained ~36% product gross margin and shipped X MW at average list ~$4,500/kW, the implied product cost (COGS) per kW would be ~$2,900/kW. The delta from list ~$4,500/kW down to customer's installed turnkey is roughly $3,500-5,000/kW after installation revenue and customer financing. These are *inferred*, not stated.
bloom_FY2024_implied_economics._action_required: Extract MW shipped from 10-K MD&A to compute true implied $/kW and update this entry.

open_questions_for_followup: Bloom Energy 10-K MD&A: FY2024 product MW shipped / acceptances — direct extraction
Bloom Energy 10-K MD&A: average selling price per unit if disclosed
DOE H2/FC TO most recent annual cost benchmark (typically published as a PDF report)
Bloom Energy 'Electricity-as-a-Service' (EaaS) PPA $/MWh ranges — Equinix REIT disclosures and similar publicly-traded customer filings
State incentive program disclosures (CA SGIP, CT FCDM) — often include $/kW for funded fuel-cell projects
